title: Competitive Programming as Mathematical Training
content: Competitive Programming as Mathematical Training Published: May 20, 2026 Last Updated: Jun 3, 2026 Version: v1.3 👁 --- Views ⏱ 12 min read 📝 2,214 words 🏷 Math 🏷 Algorithms In December 2024, I solved all 50 Advent of Code problems and ranked first at IIT Kanpur among 200+ participants. My Codeforces rating peaked at 1729 — the Hardy-Ramanujan number, which I find fitting. I have been doing competitive programming seriously since my second year, and before that, I was doing contest math through RMO, AMTI, AIMED, and KVPY. There is a popular view that competitive programming is "just implementation" or "just DSA for interviews." I disagree. Done well, competitive programming is mathematical training with a compiler as your proof checker. The Transfer from Contest Math When I was preparing for RMO and INMO in class 9-10, the problems required finding elegant constructions, proving inequalities, and reasoning about combinatorial structures. The tools were different from CP — no computers, no time limits — but the cognitive process was identical: decompose a problem, identify the key structural insight, and build a solution from that insight. In Codeforces Div 2, problems C through E follow the same pattern. The implementation is secondary. The hard part is seeing that a string problem is secretly a graph problem, or that an optimization question reduces to a binary search over the answer space. This is mathematical pattern recognition, not software engineering. Why Quant Firms Care About CP Every serious quant firm — Jane Street, HRT, Citadel, Squarepoint — weights competitive programming and mathematical problem-solving heavily in their hiring. This is not an accident. Quantitative research requires: Speed under pressure — markets move fast, and the ability to formulate and test hypotheses quickly is essential Rigorous reasoning — a trading strategy must be mathematically sound, not just empirically profitable on backtests Edge-case thinking — the adversarial nature of CP (edge cases, corner cases, wrong answer on test 47) mirrors the adversarial nature of markets Optimization instinct — reducing time complexity from O(n²) to O(n log n) is directly analogous to reducing latency in trading systems Concrete Examples Here are patterns from CP that I have seen directly in quantitative contexts: Binary search on the answer — this technique, ubiquitous in CP, is essentially how you calibrate parameters in quantitative models when the objective function is monotonic Segment trees and range queries — the exact data structures used in real-time market data processing, where you need efficient aggregation over sliding windows Dynamic programming — optimal substructure is the foundation of pricing models, portfolio optimization, and execution algorithms Graph algorithms — market networks, correlation structures, and order routing can all be modeled as graph problems The Deliberate Practice Framework My approach to CP training was systematic, not casual: After each contest, review every problem I did not solve — understand the editorial, implement it, and categorize the technique Maintain a personal problem log organized by technique (greedy, DP, graphs, number theory, etc.) Practice under timed conditions — CodeChef starters, Codeforces rounds — to build speed and stress tolerance Focus on the problems just above my current level (rated 1800-2100 when my rating was ~1700) This is the same approach I now apply to learning quantitative methods: systematic, categorized, with deliberate focus on the frontier of my understanding. If you are a student considering whether CP is "worth it" — especially if you are targeting quant roles — the answer is yes. But do it as mathematical training, not as interview prep. The depth of understanding matters more than the badge color. Contents Math Algorithms

title: The ML Systems Stack: From Data Pipelines to Inference
content: The ML Systems Stack: From Data Pipelines to Inference Published: March 15, 2026 Last Updated: Apr 12, 2026 Version: v1.2 👁 --- Views ⏱ 15 min read 📝 2,804 words 🏷 Systems 🏷 ML Most ML courses teach you to train models. Almost none teach you to run them in production. After working on HPC systems (CS633), operating systems (CS330), and multi-agent LLM pipelines (Adobe Research), I have developed opinions about what the full ML systems stack should look like. This post is an opinionated walkthrough. Layer 1: Data Ingestion Everything starts with data. The first decision — batch vs. streaming — determines the architecture of everything downstream. Batch processing is simpler and sufficient for most training workloads. Tools: Apache Spark, Dask, or even well-written Python scripts for moderate data sizes. Stream processing is necessary when features depend on real-time events — trading signals, user behavior, sensor readings. Tools: Kafka + Flink, or lighter-weight solutions like Redis Streams. The critical insight from my HPC work: I/O is almost always the bottleneck . In CS633, replacing sequential I/O with parallel MPI-IO gave us 8× speedup. The same principle applies to data pipelines — parallelize reads, use columnar formats (Parquet, Arrow), and never let your GPU sit idle waiting for data. Layer 2: Feature Engineering The gap between academic ML and production ML is largest at the feature engineering layer. In courses, features are given. In production, they must be computed, versioned, validated, and served at inference time with the same code that produced training features. Training-serving skew — where features are computed differently during training vs. inference — is the single most common source of silent model failures. Layer 3: Training Infrastructure For single-GPU training, PyTorch is sufficient. For distributed training, the stack becomes complex: Data parallelism (DDP) — each GPU trains on a different data shard, gradients are all-reduced Model parallelism — for models too large for a single GPU, split layers across devices Pipeline parallelism — overlap computation across micro-batches to improve GPU utilization From CS633, I learned that the communication pattern matters as much as the computation. Ring all-reduce, tree reduction, and gradient compression are not academic curiosities — they determine whether your training job runs in hours or days. Layer 4: Serving and Inference A model that cannot serve predictions at the required latency and throughput is useless. Key considerations: Batching — dynamic batching (collecting multiple requests and processing them together) is essential for GPU utilization Quantization — INT8/FP16 inference can halve latency with minimal accuracy loss for most models Caching — for models with repeated inputs (recommendation systems, search), caching predictions is often more efficient than recomputing Layer 5: Observability This is the layer most teams skip. A model in production without monitoring is a ticking time bomb. You need: Input distribution monitoring — detect data drift before it causes silent accuracy degradation Prediction distribution monitoring — sudden shifts in output distribution signal model issues Latency monitoring — p50, p95, p99 latencies, not just averages Business metric correlation — link model predictions to downstream outcomes What Quantitative Systems Add In quantitative finance, the ML stack has additional constraints that make everything harder: Latency requirements are measured in microseconds, not milliseconds — this rules out most standard serving frameworks Non-stationarity — financial data distributions shift constantly, requiring continuous retraining and model selection Adversarial environment — other market participants adapt to your signals, creating a moving target Regulatory constraints — explainability, audit trails, and risk limits impose architectural requirements Building ML systems is engineering work that requires both breadth and depth. If this space interests you, start with the fundamentals: operating systems (how memory and I/O actually work), networking (how data moves between machines), and distributed systems (how machines coordinate). The ML-specific layers build on top of these foundations. Contents Systems ML

title: Building Efficient Multi-Agent Systems: Lessons from Adobe Research
content: Building Efficient Multi-Agent Systems: Lessons from Adobe Research Published: April 10, 2026 Last Updated: May 1, 2026 Version: v2.0 👁 --- Views ⏱ 18 min read 📝 3,891 words 🏷 ML 🏷 LLMs During summer 2025, I worked at Adobe Research's Big Data Experience Lab on a problem that sounds deceptively simple: make multi-agent LLM systems communicate more efficiently. Three months later, we had achieved a 35% reduction in decode tokens with 1.5× latency improvements while maintaining accuracy on three benchmark suites. Here is what I learned. The Problem is Communication, Not Intelligence When most people think about improving multi-agent LLM systems, they focus on making individual agents smarter — better prompts, better models, better reasoning chains. But after systematic benchmarking of AutoGen, LangChain, DSPy, TextGrad, and AdalFlow, I found that the dominant bottleneck was not agent intelligence. It was inter-agent communication. Agents were sending each other entire conversation histories, repeating context that the receiving agent already had, and filling their context windows with information irrelevant to their specific subtask. The result: token waste, context pollution, and compounding latency. Three Key Insights 1. Context Compression Must Be Task-Aware Naive approaches to reducing communication — summarization, truncation, random sampling — all fail for the same reason: they do not know what matters for the downstream task. A summary that preserves the "main idea" might discard the specific data point that the next agent needs to make a decision. Our solution was to make the compression module aware of the task decomposition. Each agent receives only the context slice relevant to its assigned subtask. 2. Real-Time Coordination is Feasible There is a common assumption that large language models are too slow for real-time coordination. In practice, with structured communication protocols — where agents exchange compact, typed messages rather than free-form text — coordination overhead becomes manageable. The key is designing the protocol so that agents do not need to wait for complete responses. Partial results can be streamed and consumed incrementally. 3. Evaluation Must Be Multi-Dimensional We validated on HotpotQA (multi-hop QA), GPQA (graduate-level reasoning), and GSM8K (mathematical problem-solving). No single benchmark captures the full picture. A system that excels at QA might fail at code generation. Multi-dimensional evaluation is not a luxury — it is a requirement. What I Would Do Differently Start with profiling, not optimization. We spent the first two weeks running experiments without a clear bottleneck analysis. The systematic profiling phase, when we finally did it, revealed that 60% of tokens were redundant — and pointed us directly at the right intervention Build evaluation infrastructure early. Ad-hoc evaluation slows everything down. Automating benchmark runs from day one would have saved weeks Read more broadly. The most useful insight — task-aware routing — came from the distributed systems literature, not the LLM literature. Cross-pollination between fields is underrated Connections to Quantitative Systems Many of these lessons transfer directly to quantitative trading systems: Communication overhead between components (data feeds, signal generators, risk managers, execution engines) is a constant concern Task-aware routing is analogous to routing market data only to the strategies that need it Multi-dimensional evaluation mirrors the need to evaluate strategies across multiple market regimes The full technical details are in the project page . For questions about multi-agent systems or research methodology — feel free to reach out. Contents ML LLMs
